_note: This seed curriculum is designed to train general creative cognition rather than domain memorization. It keeps a small simple core for fast tests and short traces, while most prompts push toward ambiguity, tension, structural branching, recombination, and non-obvious synthesis. The hackathon-style prompt belongs in held-out evaluation, not the training curriculum.

everyday_creative.label: Everyday Creative Framing
prompts: Find a name for my sausage dog that feels distinctive, a little unexpected, and still affectionate.
Come up with a birthday present for someone who says they want nothing, but make it meaningful rather than expensive.
Design a dinner party theme for 8 people who have nothing in common, and make the format itself create connection.
Invent a rainy-day game for two bored children using only paper and tape, but it should get better after the first round.
Create a morning ritual for someone who hates routines but wants to feel more imaginative before work.
Redesign a thank-you note for someone you barely know so it feels sincere without sounding generic.
Design a new way to celebrate a personal failure so it feels like a genuine milestone rather than a pity party.
Create a system for a shared household fridge that reduces friction without using labels or rigid rules.
Invent a goodbye ritual for ending a friendship that has naturally run its course, without making either person feel abandoned.
Design a way for two people to split a restaurant bill fairly without anyone feeling tracked or judged.
Create a physical activity that two people with very different fitness levels can do together and both genuinely enjoy.
Invent a gift for someone who already owns everything they want but experiences life through texture and sound.
Design a weekly family meeting that teenagers would actually want to attend, not just tolerate.
Create a journaling practice for someone who hates writing but needs to process difficult emotions.
Invent a way to re-introduce yourself to a city you grew up in but now find unfamiliar.
Design a quiet celebration for an introvert who has just achieved something significant.
Create a system for remembering strangers' names without it feeling like a memory trick.
Invent a way to give constructive feedback to a friend about their creative work without damaging the friendship.
Design a shared reading experience for two people on opposite ends of the world in different time zones.
Create a meaningful first-day-of-school ritual for a child who is anxious about new environments.
Invent a way to apologize for something small that happened years ago without making it feel bigger than it is.
Design a dinner tradition that forces family members to share something they learned, not just what happened.
Create a personal metric for measuring whether a day was creative, that has nothing to do with output.
Invent a way for neighbors who have never spoken to begin a natural, non-awkward relationship.
Design a technique for ending a phone call with a relative that is honest without being hurtful.
Create a structure for a solo trip to a city you know well that still generates genuine surprise.
Invent a daily practice that cultivates patience without using meditation or breathing exercises.
Design a method for two estranged siblings to reconnect without addressing the original conflict directly.
Create a habit that helps someone process anger before it damages a relationship.
Invent a birthday tradition for someone who finds birthdays existentially uncomfortable.
Design a way to make household chores feel genuinely satisfying rather than just done.
Create a ritual for the end of a workday that cleanly separates professional and personal identity.
Invent a gift that forces the recipient to do something they have been afraid to try.
Design a game for a long car journey that surfaces surprising things about the people in the car.
Create a way to make a difficult conversation easier by changing the physical environment, not the words.
Invent a weekly habit that builds creative confidence without requiring any artistic skill.
Design a farewell ceremony for leaving a job that you loved but had to leave.
Create a personal rule for deciding when to say yes and when to say no that does not rely on mood.
Invent a way to commemorate a difficult year that honors the struggle without dwelling in it.
Design a shared workspace arrangement for two people with opposite working styles who must share a room.
Create a method for turning an embarrassing memory into something you can tell as a story with pride.
Invent a way to end a habit you love that is slowly harming you without replacing it with another habit.
Design a Sunday routine for someone who dreads Monday without using productivity hacks.
Create a way to disagree with someone you respect without either person feeling dismissed.
Invent a tradition for a couple to mark every anniversary that becomes more meaningful with each repetition.
Design an activity that two people can do together that requires no shared language.
Create a method for choosing what to read next that avoids both algorithm recommendations and peer pressure.
Invent a way to transform a mundane commute into a reliable source of creative inspiration.

naming_branding.label: Naming & Identity Design
prompts: Name an AI-powered creative studio for musicians, but avoid futuristic cliches and obvious music words.
Create a brand name for a biotech startup in gut microbiome therapeutics that sounds trustworthy, not sterile.
Name a nonprofit that teaches critical thinking to teenagers through debate without sounding academic or preachy.
Rebrand a 100-year-old public library for the digital age while preserving its civic dignity.
Name a multilingual education platform for refugee communities that feels locally human, not NGO generic.
Invent a name for a new discipline combining neuroscience, design, and indigenous knowledge systems, and make it feel like a real field.
Name a high-end restaurant where the menu is dictated entirely by local foraging, avoiding words like rustic or wild.
Brand a radical honesty dating app without using the words truth, real, or bare.
Name a cooperative housing project for people in their 30s who value both privacy and community, avoiding commune or co-living cliches.
Create a brand identity for a luxury water brand whose entire proposition is conscious restraint, not indulgence.
Name a law firm that specializes in representing whistleblowers without using words like justice, truth, or courage.
Brand a children's science museum that makes adults feel equally welcome, without dumbing anything down.
Name a meditation app built specifically for people who have tried meditation and failed, without being condescending.
Create a name for a new professional certification in ethical AI governance that feels serious but not bureaucratic.
Name a peer-to-peer lending platform for artisans in developing economies that feels dignified, not charitable.
Brand a coworking space for solopreneurs that is designed to combat loneliness, not just provide desks.
Name a search engine that returns only long-form, peer-reviewed, or primary-source content.
Create a brand for a clothing line that is designed to last 20 years, not seasons.
Name a podcast network that exclusively covers slow, complex stories that take months to unfold.
Brand a grocery delivery service whose entire model is based on ugly or surplus produce.
Name a platform that helps scientists communicate their work to non-specialists without simplifying the findings.
Create a name for a new genre of travel that is explicitly not tourism and not digital nomadism.
Name a professional network for people who have voluntarily stepped back from high-status careers.
Brand a mental health platform for men in industries where vulnerability is actively punished.
Name an insurance product designed for gig workers that adapts to irregular income without punishing gaps.
Create a name for a civic initiative that makes local government meetings genuinely interesting to attend.
Name a dating platform whose matching algorithm is based on values conflict rather than compatibility.
Brand a physical newspaper that is printed once a month with only the five most important stories.
Name a cooking school that teaches people to cook one extraordinary dish per year, not a full repertoire.
Create a name for a digital archive that preserves the everyday writing of ordinary people, not public figures.
Name a consulting firm that only works with organizations willing to be publicly transparent about their failures.
Brand a hospital system whose primary metric is time spent with patients rather than throughput.
Name a new academic journal that publishes only negative or null results from scientific experiments.
Create a name for a city program that pays residents to stay in their neighborhood rather than gentrify it.
Name a hardware device that forces users to fully shut down and restart their digital habits every 90 days.
Brand a financial advisory service that exclusively helps people simplify their finances rather than grow them.
Name a global network of libraries that operate as community centers in places without conventional community infrastructure.
Create a name for a platform that matches people with mentors who have failed significantly in their field.
Name a startup that sells nothing but curates other companies' products with extreme editorial restraint.
Brand a professional association for people who have left academia to work in non-traditional settings.
Name a service that helps aging parents and adult children have structured conversations about death and legacy.
Create a brand for a co-parenting coordination tool that is designed around reducing conflict, not increasing efficiency.
Name an urban farming initiative that operates entirely on reclaimed land with no government subsidy.
Brand a news organization that publishes corrections more prominently than the original articles.
Name a performance venue that exclusively features artists who are performing publicly for the first time.
Create a name for a sleep health platform that operates without tracking devices or data collection.
Name a recruitment agency that specializes in placing people into second careers after significant life disruption.
Brand a social platform that automatically deletes posts after 30 days, with no option to archive.

teaching_explanation.label: Teaching & Cognitive Tool Design
prompts: Explain recursion to a 12-year-old who hates math, but make the explanation itself memorable enough to retell.
Design a 10-minute lesson that makes students feel the difference between correlation and causation rather than just hearing it.
Teach photosynthesis using only kitchen objects, but avoid the usual factory analogy.
Create a way to explain inflation to adults with no economics background without using money metaphors.
Design an exercise that helps students notice their own hidden assumptions while solving a problem.
Invent a visual notation for teaching feedback loops to teenagers so they can use it across subjects.
Explain the concept of compound interest to a high schooler by using an analogy of a spreading rumor.
Design a physical classroom game that perfectly illustrates the prisoner's dilemma.
Teach the concept of entropy to a 15-year-old using only events that happen in a typical kitchen.
Design a classroom exercise that makes the difference between a law and a theory feel viscerally real.
Explain why most startups fail without using business jargon, to a recent graduate with no business background.
Create a tactile demonstration of how vaccines train the immune system that uses no syringes or medical props.
Design a lesson that teaches statistical significance without using numbers or graphs in the first 10 minutes.
Explain how compilers work to someone who has only ever used a microwave and a blender.
Teach the concept of opportunity cost to a 10-year-old using a Saturday morning as the unit of analysis.
Design an exercise that helps teenagers understand power dynamics in a conversation without role-playing.
Explain the difference between sympathy and empathy using only physical actions and no abstract words.
Create a lesson that makes debt feel real and consequential to students who have never borrowed money.
Design an activity that teaches second-order thinking to adults who tend to stop at first-order consequences.
Explain how machine learning models generalize without using the words data, training, or prediction.
Teach the concept of diminishing returns using an experience the students will actually have this week.
Design a demonstration that makes confirmation bias visible to people who believe they are immune to it.
Explain how the immune system distinguishes self from non-self to a primary school student without using biology vocabulary.
Create a hands-on activity that teaches negotiation skills without framing it as a negotiation exercise.
Design a lesson that makes students deeply uncomfortable with the concept of sunk cost before explaining it.
Explain the concept of externalities using a story about neighbors and a barbecue.
Teach network effects to a small business owner who is skeptical of technology, using physical objects only.
Design an exercise that forces students to experience the limits of their own working memory.
Explain how trust is built in relationships to a teenager using the structure of a bank account.
Create a demonstration of path dependency using only decisions made in the past 24 hours.
Design a lesson about cognitive load that works by inducing it in real time rather than describing it.
Explain the difference between precision and accuracy to an eight-year-old using darts and arrows.
Teach the concept of asymmetric information using a used car sale and two people with different knowledge.
Design an exercise that makes implicit bias visible without making participants defensive.
Explain natural selection to someone who is actively skeptical of evolution, using domestic dog breeds.
Create a kinesthetic activity that teaches students how ecosystems balance without using the word ecosystem.
Design a lesson that makes the concept of compounding failure feel as vivid as compounding interest.
Explain why sleep matters for memory consolidation using a metaphor that involves filing a messy office.
Teach risk literacy to adults who make everyday financial decisions using grocery shopping as the domain.
Design a classroom exercise that makes students feel the difference between data and information.
Explain how social norms are enforced without laws using only examples from a school cafeteria.
Create a thought experiment that teaches the limits of individual rationality without mentioning economics.
Design a lesson that reveals how language shapes thought using two words for the same concept.
Explain the tragedy of the commons using a shared refrigerator in a student dormitory.
Teach the concept of emergence using the way a crowd at a sporting event creates a wave.
Design an activity that teaches students to distinguish between correlation and causation using social media data.
Explain how bureaucracies develop defensive behaviors without using political science terms.
Create a lesson plan that teaches the concept of diminishing marginal utility using pizza slices and regret.

product_service_ideation.label: Multi-Constraint Product & Service Design
prompts: Design an offline literacy tool for rural classrooms with unreliable electricity that still feels magical to use.
Invent a service that helps first-generation university students navigate bureaucracy without making them feel managed.
Design a peer-learning system for classrooms with 60+ students and one teacher, where the bottleneck becomes a feature.
Propose a novel approach to vaccine distribution tracking that works without smartphones, stable internet, or centralized trust.
Create a mental health support system for contexts where therapy is culturally stigmatized, but do not frame it as therapy.
Design a system that helps elderly people preserve life stories without typing, and makes the stories easier to revisit than to record.
Invent a service that turns long daily commutes on public transit into a highly desired part of the day.
Design a smart home device that intentionally introduces small, delightful inefficiencies to combat hyper-optimization.
Design a grief support service that works in cultures where public mourning is considered inappropriate.
Invent a civic tool that helps residents identify who actually makes decisions in their city, not just who holds titles.
Design a financial product for families in subsistence economies that builds resilience without requiring savings.
Invent a reading program for incarcerated people that connects them to outside intellectual communities without internet.
Design a crisis communication system for rural communities during natural disasters where phone infrastructure fails.
Invent a professional development system for teachers in overcrowded schools with no time or budget for training.
Design a neighborhood food-sharing system that eliminates food waste without creating charity dynamics.
Invent a service that helps informal workers in cities formalize enough to access services without losing flexibility.
Design a tool that helps couples in long-distance relationships maintain practical household coordination, not just emotional connection.
Invent a way for patients with chronic illness to track symptom patterns that doctors will actually read and use.
Design a service that helps adult children care for aging parents without displacing the parent's sense of autonomy.
Invent a civic feedback mechanism that local governments can use that is faster than elections and more reliable than surveys.
Design a language learning tool for elderly people with early-stage cognitive decline that slows deterioration.
Invent a conflict resolution tool for communities where the justice system is either absent or mistrusted.
Design a physical product that helps people with ADHD complete administrative tasks without requiring sustained focus.
Invent a tool for helping humanitarian organizations coordinate in the first 72 hours of a disaster without centralized command.
Design a service that helps college students with no professional network build genuine relationships with practitioners.
Invent a local archive system for a community whose history has been systematically excluded from official records.
Design a product that helps shift workers maintain health habits when their schedule changes every week.
Invent a professional mentorship program for skilled tradespeople that does not import the status hierarchies of corporate mentorship.
Design a service that helps newly arrived immigrants navigate informal social norms without making them feel surveilled.
Invent a financial planning tool for people with highly irregular incomes who experience money in feast and famine cycles.
Design a product that helps people with severe social anxiety participate in professional networking events.
Invent a feedback system for frontline healthcare workers to surface operational problems to hospital leadership without career risk.
Design a civic literacy program for new voters that builds genuine political agency rather than civic compliance.
Invent a service that helps people return to work after extended caregiving leaves without feeling professionally obsolete.
Design a community mental health resource for communities where the closest psychiatric facility is more than three hours away.
Invent a peer support system for founders of failing startups that does not feel like a support group.
Design a transit system for a mid-size city where most residents work non-traditional hours.
Invent a tool that helps freelancers scope projects accurately so they stop undercharging for their time.
Design a product that allows elderly people in assisted living to maintain genuine financial autonomy.
Invent a cross-cultural communication tool for international teams where language competence varies dramatically.
Design a service that helps people negotiate salary for the first time without feeling like they are making demands.
Invent a system that helps parents of children with rare diseases connect with the right specialists globally.
Design a product that makes it easy for people to reduce their digital footprint without technical expertise.
Invent a service for people trying to leave high-control religious communities without losing their entire social support system.
Design a community initiative that reduces food insecurity in wealthy neighborhoods where the cause is not poverty but isolation.
Invent a professional portfolio system for people whose most important work is invisible, like caregiving or community organizing.
Design a service that helps elderly people with dementia maintain a sense of identity through curated environmental cues.
Invent a tool that helps social workers document the systemic patterns they observe without adding to their administrative burden.

scientific_hypothesis.label: Scientific Hypothesis & Research Design
prompts: Generate competing hypotheses for why a promising Alzheimer's drug trial failed despite strong preclinical results, and make each imply a different next experiment.
Propose alternative mechanisms for the placebo effect in pain management that would change how trials are designed.
Generate hypotheses for why identical twins sometimes develop different autoimmune diseases, and identify what data would discriminate among them.
Suggest non-obvious explanations for why sleep deprivation harms creativity more than analytical reasoning.
Propose rival explanations for why some ecosystems recover after fire while others tip into collapse.
Invent a hypothesis for a phenomenon that looks contradictory at first glance, then describe the hidden variable that could reconcile it.
Generate hypotheses for why a specific species of bird thrives only in the noise of a dense metropolis, contrary to all ecological models.
Propose an alternative explanation for the Fermi Paradox that assumes intelligent life actively hides for a specific, logical reason.
Generate hypotheses for why some people experience time as moving faster as they age while others do not, and what psychological variable distinguishes them.
Propose alternative explanations for why urban populations in wealthy countries are experiencing declining fertility rates that do not center economic anxiety.
Generate competing hypotheses for why children raised bilingual show measurable cognitive advantages in tasks unrelated to language.
Propose mechanisms by which prolonged screen exposure at night might affect circadian rhythms through pathways beyond blue light suppression of melatonin.
Generate hypotheses for why some patients with identical tumor genetics respond dramatically differently to the same immunotherapy drug.
Propose rival explanations for why some cultures developed alphabetic writing while others developed logographic systems, and what this implies about cognition.
Generate hypotheses for why some people can hear music in their heads with perfect pitch while most cannot, without using the concept of musical training.
Propose alternative mechanisms for why grief and social rejection activate similar neural pain pathways.
Generate competing hypotheses for why the global prevalence of myopia has doubled in the past 50 years.
Propose hypotheses for why certain mathematical proofs feel aesthetically beautiful to mathematicians, and whether this aesthetic tracks truth.
Generate hypotheses for why stress increases susceptibility to the common cold, with each hypothesis implying a different immunological intervention.
Propose rival explanations for why some species of animals show evidence of grief while others do not.
Generate hypotheses for why expertise in one domain occasionally transfers to a completely unrelated domain in ways that cannot be explained by common cognitive strategies.
Propose mechanisms by which the gut microbiome could influence decision-making through pathways not involving the vagus nerve.
Generate competing hypotheses for why deep-sea organisms that have never seen light produce bioluminescent signals.
Propose hypotheses for why some people experience deja vu frequently while others report never having experienced it.
Generate rival explanations for why placebo surgery sometimes outperforms real surgery for certain orthopedic conditions.
Propose alternative mechanisms for why meditation produces measurable changes in cortical thickness in regions associated with attention.
Generate hypotheses for why languages spoken in geographically isolated communities evolve faster than those in connected ones.
Propose rival explanations for why some people are constitutionally resistant to persuasion regardless of argument quality.
Generate hypotheses for why identical inputs to a chaotic system produce diverging outputs, with each hypothesis implying a different computational model.
Propose mechanisms for why childhood trauma can produce physical health consequences that manifest decades later.
Generate competing hypotheses for why social animals sleep in synchronized patterns and what the evolutionary function of synchrony might be.
Propose alternative explanations for why some people recover full function after traumatic brain injury while others with less severe damage do not.
Generate hypotheses for why disgust sensitivity correlates with conservative political attitudes in some cultures but not in others.
Propose rival mechanisms for how the brain consolidates emotionally charged memories differently from neutral ones.
Generate hypotheses for why the placebo effect is stronger in some delivery contexts than others, controlling for patient expectations.
Propose alternative explanations for why certain musical scales produce similar emotional responses across unrelated musical traditions.
Generate competing hypotheses for why languages almost universally use rising intonation for questions, even when the grammar does not require it.
Propose mechanisms by which social isolation might accelerate biological aging independent of behavioral risk factors like smoking and diet.
Generate hypotheses for why some populations with low average caloric intake show better longevity outcomes than populations with higher caloric intake.
Propose rival explanations for why children under five can learn any language effortlessly but the window closes so sharply.
Generate hypotheses for why some coral reef systems in marginal conditions prove more resilient than expected to warming events.
Propose mechanisms for how cities generate disproportionate levels of innovation relative to their population size.
Generate competing hypotheses for why pain tolerance varies so dramatically between individuals with similar physiological profiles.
Propose alternative explanations for why species at the top of food chains are more vulnerable to extinction than species in the middle.
Generate hypotheses for why some neurons in the adult brain retain the capacity for plasticity while the majority do not.
Propose rival mechanisms by which chronic low-grade inflammation might predispose individuals to depression.
Generate hypotheses for why voluntary simplicity movements consistently fail to scale beyond a committed minority even when participants report high satisfaction.
Propose alternative explanations for why some mathematical constructs discovered for purely abstract reasons later turn out to describe physical phenomena precisely.

strategic_reframing.label: Strategic Reframing & Problem Redefinition
prompts: Reframe 'how do we increase school attendance?' as a systems problem, but give three definitions of success that conflict with each other.
Reframe 'how do we reduce hospital readmissions?' by questioning what a good discharge outcome really is.
Find three alternative definitions of productivity that would completely change what a company optimizes for.
Reframe 'how do we fight misinformation?' without assuming that more facts are the solution.
Reframe 'how do we make AI safer?' from the viewpoint of three stakeholders whose values genuinely conflict.
Reframe 'how do we solve burnout?' without centering the individual as the main unit of change.
Reframe 'how do we stop employee turnover?' into a problem where turnover is actually a healthy goal.
Reframe 'how do we eliminate traffic congestion?' without adding more roads or reducing the number of cars.
Reframe 'how do we make cities safer?' without centering surveillance, policing, or infrastructure.
Reframe 'how do we reduce inequality?' by questioning whether reduction is actually the most useful goal.
Find three alternative definitions of innovation that would make most Silicon Valley companies look uninnovative.
Reframe 'how do we close the gender pay gap?' by questioning whether pay is the right unit of measurement.
Reframe 'how do we improve public transit?' from the perspective of someone who has never owned a car and uses it every day.
Reframe 'how do we reduce plastic waste?' without assuming the solution involves consumer behavior change.
Find three alternative definitions of success for a hospital that would change what gets measured and who gets promoted.
Reframe 'how do we fix education?' by questioning whether the current form of schooling is the thing being fixed.
Reframe 'how do we reduce loneliness in cities?' by identifying what cities already do that creates loneliness as a structural outcome.
Find three alternative definitions of security that would change how a national government allocates its entire defense budget.
Reframe 'how do we improve public health?' by removing the individual as the primary unit of intervention.
Reframe 'how do we make workplaces more inclusive?' by questioning what inclusion actually means for the people being included.
Find three definitions of poverty that would make current anti-poverty programs look counterproductive.
Reframe 'how do we reduce screen time in children?' without treating screens as the problem.
Reframe 'how do we make democracy work better?' from the perspective that participation rate is a misleading success metric.
Find three alternative definitions of health that would completely change what a person optimizes for on a daily basis.
Reframe 'how do we reduce youth unemployment?' by questioning whether employment is the right outcome to optimize for.
Reframe 'how do we stop climate change?' by questioning who the 'we' is and what power they actually have.
Find three alternative definitions of justice that would make the current legal system look like a failure on its own terms.
Reframe 'how do we fix broken supply chains?' without assuming globalization is the correct baseline.
Reframe 'how do we help people make better financial decisions?' by questioning what better means in this context.
Find three alternative definitions of community that would make most social media platforms look actively destructive.
Reframe 'how do we reduce opioid addiction?' by questioning the assumption that the goal is abstinence.
Reframe 'how do we build better AI?' from the perspective of communities most likely to be harmed by it.
Find three alternative definitions of expertise that would change who organizations listen to when making major decisions.
Reframe 'how do we solve the housing crisis?' by questioning who is defining crisis and in whose interest.
Reframe 'how do we improve corporate culture?' by questioning whether culture can be changed top-down at all.
Find three alternative definitions of risk that would change how insurance companies design their products from scratch.
Reframe 'how do we reduce organizational silos?' by questioning whether silos are actually a problem in all contexts.
Reframe 'how do we make science more accessible?' by questioning the direction of the information flow.
Find three alternative definitions of learning that would make most corporate training programs look like active interference.
Reframe 'how do we address food insecurity?' by questioning whether the problem is scarcity or distribution of existing abundance.
Reframe 'how do we reduce violence in schools?' by questioning whether the school is the right unit of intervention.
Find three alternative definitions of privacy that would change what a technology company is legally required to protect.
Reframe 'how do we help people exercise more?' by removing willpower and motivation as causal variables.
Reframe 'how do we make cities more sustainable?' by questioning what cities are actually optimizing for right now.
Find three alternative definitions of waste that would make most manufacturing processes look fundamentally different.
Reframe 'how do we reduce corporate fraud?' by questioning whether the current enforcement model creates the conditions for it.
Reframe 'how do we help people be happier?' by questioning whether happiness is an appropriate optimization target.
Find three alternative definitions of leadership that would make most leadership development programs look actively counterproductive.

cross_domain_analogy.label: Deep Structural Analogy
prompts: Find a deep structural analogy between immune systems and market regulation, and say where the analogy breaks.
Map jazz improvisation onto software architecture, but avoid surface-level 'flexibility' comparisons.
Draw a structural analogy between coral reefs and open-source communities, and identify one misleading overlap.
Find parallels between forest recovery after fire and how teams recover from failure, but focus on system dynamics rather than emotion words.
Map the structure of a legal appeals process onto machine learning model refinement.
Find an analogy between mycelial nutrient sharing and how tacit knowledge moves inside organizations, then show what the analogy cannot explain.
Find a structural analogy between the formation of a black hole and a company undergoing rapid hyper-growth.
Map the process of distilling a fine whiskey onto the process of editing a messy first draft of a novel.
Map the structure of a criminal trial onto the process of debugging a complex software system, and identify the point where the analogy fails most dramatically.
Find a structural analogy between the way a river delta forms and how a startup ecosystem develops in a city.
Map the process of training an athlete for a specific event onto the process of training a neural network, and identify what the analogy hides about both.
Draw a structural analogy between diplomatic negotiation and the process of merging two codebases with conflicting architectures.
Find parallels between how diseases spread through a social network and how ideas spread through an academic field.
Map the structure of a coral bleaching event onto the dynamics of an employee exodus from a company with a toxic culture.
Find a structural analogy between the immune system's tolerance of self-antigens and the way a legal system handles conflicts of interest.
Draw a structural analogy between how a conductor leads an orchestra and how a product manager leads a cross-functional team, then identify the misleading overlap.
Map the process of translating a poem from one language to another onto the process of porting software to a new platform.
Find parallels between how cities develop informal economies and how the body develops compensatory mechanisms after injury.
Map the structure of a food web onto the structure of an information ecosystem, and identify where the analogy breaks down completely.
Draw a structural analogy between how a glacier moves and how institutional change happens in a large bureaucracy.
Find a structural analogy between the way memory consolidates during sleep and the way a version control system handles merges.
Map the ecology of a tidal pool onto the dynamics of a small creative team under external pressure.
Find parallels between how epidemics cross geographical boundaries and how financial contagion crosses regulatory boundaries.
Map the structure of a parliamentary debate onto the process of code review in an open-source project.
Draw a structural analogy between how a language evolves through contact with other languages and how an organization's culture changes through mergers.
Find parallels between the way dark matter influences galactic structure without being detectable and how informal power structures influence organizational decisions.
Map the structure of a chess endgame onto a business negotiation where one party has significantly more resources.
Find a structural analogy between the way vaccines train the immune system and the way simulations train pilots.
Draw a structural analogy between how a city's road network becomes congested and how a database becomes slow under high concurrency.
Map the process of fermentation onto the process of peer review in academic publishing.
Find parallels between how a keystone species structures an ecosystem and how a single infrastructure company structures a technology market.
Map the dynamics of a hostage negotiation onto the dynamics of a customer support escalation in a high-stakes B2B relationship.
Find a structural analogy between how erosion reshapes a coastline and how user feedback reshapes a software product over time.
Draw a structural analogy between the way a symphony is orchestrated and the way a complex legislation is drafted through committee.
Map the process of taxonomic classification in biology onto the process of organizing a company's knowledge management system.
Find parallels between how a fire uses available fuel and how a social movement uses available grievance.
Map the structure of a legal appeal onto the structure of a scientific replication study, and identify where the goals diverge.
Find a structural analogy between how invasive species disrupt ecosystems and how a monopoly platform disrupts a competitive market.
Draw a structural analogy between how the brain prunes unused neural pathways in adolescence and how a company restructures after rapid growth.
Map the process of dead reckoning in navigation onto the process of forecasting revenue in a new market.
Find parallels between how a language creates untranslatable words for specific experiences and how a domain of expertise creates jargon that resists simplification.
Map the structure of a search and rescue operation onto the structure of an incident response team handling a major software outage.
Find a structural analogy between how a coral reef creates habitat complexity and how a platform company creates a developer ecosystem.
Draw a structural analogy between how a seed remains dormant until conditions are right and how a social movement waits for its precipitating event.
Map the dynamics of a mutualistic relationship between a cleaner fish and its host onto the dynamics of a vendor-client relationship where both parties are dependent.
Find parallels between how a river finds the path of least resistance and how bureaucratic processes route around internal resistance.
Map the structure of biological metamorphosis onto the process of a company undergoing a successful business model pivot.
Find a structural analogy between the way quantum superposition collapses upon observation and the way organizational strategy becomes fixed once communicated publicly.

narrative_worldbuilding.label: Narrative & Rule-Based Worldbuilding
prompts: Create a villain whose worldview is internally coherent and compassionate, but still dangerous.
Design a non-human character with alien cognition that is not just human emotion in strange clothing.
Build a protagonist defined by a contradiction that makes them effective in one context and destructive in another.
Design a story world where one physical law works differently, then trace the second-order social consequences.
Create a mentor whose advice is sincerely wise inside one moral framework and systematically harmful inside another.
Invent a city whose architecture reveals its political system better than any speech could.
Design a society where memory is a currency that can be traded, and describe the new forms of poverty it creates.
Create a world where gravity fluctuates predictably based on the moon, and trace the impact on its architecture.
Design a society where people are born with a fixed number of words they can speak over their lifetime, and describe the social hierarchies this creates.
Create a character who is defined by a compulsion to fix things but whose solutions always create worse problems than the originals.
Build a world where all art is created collaboratively and individual attribution is illegal, then trace how power consolidates anyway.
Design a culture where lying is physically impossible but deception through truth-telling has become an art form.
Create a protagonist whose greatest strength is their capacity for forgiveness, then put them in a situation where forgiveness is the most destructive choice.
Build a world where technology stopped advancing 200 years ago but social organization has continued to evolve, then trace the tensions.
Design a villain who is entirely motivated by a genuine desire to eliminate suffering, but whose definition of suffering is slightly different from everyone else's.
Create a character whose defining trait is that they remember every conversation they have ever had, and explore what this does to their relationships.
Build a society where status is determined entirely by the number of people you have genuinely helped, and describe the gaming that develops.
Design a world where music is the primary legal language and all contracts and laws must be encoded in it.
Create a protagonist who is the last practitioner of a craft that has no living audience left to appreciate it.
Build a world where aging is reversed after 40 and people become younger until they disappear, then trace the social consequences.
Design a culture where conflict is resolved exclusively through competitive storytelling, and describe the figure of the professional story-fighter.
Create a character who has been exiled from every community they have ever tried to join, not for bad behavior but for being too good.
Build a world where the physical laws of cause and effect are reversed in small, unpredictable increments, and describe how science adapted.
Design a society that has eliminated privacy entirely but retains shame as a social mechanism.
Create a character who is incapable of changing their mind, but who is not stupid and must navigate a world that requires adaptation.
Build a world where dreams are public and can be reviewed by others, then describe what this does to the concept of the unconscious.
Design a culture that worships failure as sacred and treats success with deep suspicion.
Create a protagonist who discovers that every decision they make is already known by someone else, but cannot determine if this is surveillance or prophecy.
Build a world where language degraded and people have developed entirely new communication systems based on gesture and color.
Design a society where children raise their parents rather than the reverse, and trace the political structures that emerge.
Create a character who is perfectly rational and whose rationality makes them incapable of connecting with anyone around them.
Build a world where technology can preserve consciousness indefinitely but the law defines you as dead after 120 years regardless.
Design a culture where all information is oral and the written word is considered a form of violence against memory.
Create a character who experiences time in reverse only emotionally, meaning they grieve things before they happen and celebrate losses.
Build a world where political power is inherited matrilineally but economic power is controlled entirely by unmarried men, and trace the result.
Design a society where all citizens are required to change their name every decade, and describe the identity technologies that develop.
Create a protagonist who is the most trustworthy person in a world where trust has been eliminated as a social concept.
Build a world where the climate requires humans to live underground, but surface expeditions are treated as religious pilgrimages.
Design a culture where art can only be made by people over 70, and trace how this shapes cultural transmission.
Create a character whose superpower is that they are impossible to remember, and explore whether this constitutes freedom or imprisonment.
Build a world where the dominant religion worships the future rather than the past, and trace the political consequences.
Design a society where the highest social honor is to be forgotten, and describe the bureaucracy of managed erasure.
Create a villain who genuinely loves the protagonist and believes the harm they are causing is the kindest possible gift.
Build a world where every person is born with a twin who lives the life the person did not choose.
Design a culture where conflict is resolved by the conflicting parties swapping lives for 30 days, and trace what this does to injustice.
Create a protagonist who is the only person in their world who has read all the books that were burned in a historical purge.
Build a world where the immune system can be voluntarily tuned by practitioners, and describe both the medicine and the politics.
Design a society where sleep is public and all citizens sleep in communal chambers, then trace the private and public dynamics.

philosophical_reasoning.label: Philosophical Thought Experiments
prompts: Construct a thought experiment that challenges the hard problem of consciousness without using the standard examples.
Reframe the trolley problem through a non-Western ethical framework, and show what the original setup hides.
Design a thought experiment that reveals a hidden assumption in how we define intelligence.
Construct an argument that makes a widely held moral intuition feel unstable rather than simply wrong.
Invent a new paradox about identity that could only arise in a networked, AI-mediated society.
Challenge the assumption that authenticity is always a virtue by creating a case where performance is ethically superior.
Design a thought experiment that questions whether extreme longevity would actually destroy the concept of urgency.
Challenge the idea that empathy is always good by constructing a scenario where perfect empathy leads to total paralysis.
Design a thought experiment that makes the concept of free will feel incoherent from inside a deterministic framework, without assuming physicalism.
Construct an argument that personal identity is a harmful concept without reducing identity to physical continuity.
Design a thought experiment that reveals why justice and fairness are not the same thing and sometimes actively conflict.
Challenge the assumption that rational actors make better decisions by constructing a case where irrationality is the superior strategy.
Construct a paradox about consent that arises specifically from situations where future preferences cannot be known.
Design a thought experiment that makes the concept of moral progress feel circular rather than linear.
Challenge the assumption that transparency is always an epistemic virtue by constructing a case where it is epistemically harmful.
Construct an argument that reveals the hidden assumption in the claim that suffering should be eliminated rather than managed.
Design a thought experiment that makes the concept of desert, the idea that people deserve what they get, feel incoherent.
Challenge the assumption that living longer is unconditionally valuable without using quality of life as a counter-argument.
Construct a paradox about memory that only arises when the entity remembering is a collective rather than an individual.
Design a thought experiment that makes the concept of privacy feel incoherent when applied to thoughts that have not been shared.
Challenge the assumption that honesty is a virtue by constructing a case where strategic deception produces better epistemic outcomes for everyone involved.
Construct an argument that the concept of informed consent is philosophically unstable in the context of irreversible decisions.
Design a thought experiment that reveals a contradiction in the idea of cultural relativism as a moral framework.
Challenge the assumption that consistency is a virtue in moral reasoning by constructing a case where principled inconsistency is ethically superior.
Construct a paradox about expertise that arises specifically in domains where the experts and the affected communities have structurally opposed interests.
Design a thought experiment that makes the distinction between helping and controlling feel impossible to maintain.
Challenge the assumption that intentions matter morally by constructing a case where identical intentions produce outcomes that demand opposite moral evaluations.
Construct an argument that the concept of responsibility becomes incoherent when causal chains extend across generations.
Design a thought experiment that makes the concept of fairness feel dependent on a prior agreement that was never actually made.
Challenge the assumption that coherence is a virtue in a belief system by constructing a case where incoherence is protective.
Construct a paradox about knowledge that arises when the act of knowing changes the thing being known.
Design a thought experiment that reveals why treating people as ends rather than means is easier to say than to do in systems-level decisions.
Challenge the assumption that democracy requires equal votes by constructing a case where unequal votes produce more democratically legitimate outcomes.
Construct an argument that reveals the hidden assumption in the claim that science is value-free.
Design a thought experiment that makes the concept of ownership feel philosophically fragile when applied to ideas.
Challenge the assumption that education is always an expansion of freedom by constructing a case where it systematically narrows possibilities.
Construct a paradox about trust that arises specifically in systems designed to verify trustworthiness.
Design a thought experiment that reveals what moral intuitions about punishment are actually tracking when they resist consequentialist justification.
Challenge the assumption that more information always improves decision-making by constructing a case where it produces systematic errors.
Construct an argument that the concept of human rights is either redundant or incoherent depending on the metaethical framework used to ground it.
Design a thought experiment that makes the concept of a fair process feel independent of the concept of a fair outcome.
Challenge the assumption that cooperation is naturally preferable to competition by constructing a case where forced cooperation produces worse outcomes than structured adversarial relationships.
Construct a paradox about authority that arises when the legitimacy of the authority depends on the consent of those who cannot yet know what they are consenting to.
Design a thought experiment that makes the distinction between killing and letting die feel morally irrelevant in one specific but generalizable case.
Challenge the assumption that self-interest is always the enemy of collective good by constructing a case where it is structurally necessary for collective welfare.
Construct an argument that the concept of merit is always parasitic on prior decisions about what counts as merit.
Design a thought experiment that reveals why the concept of a right implies a corresponding obligation that is not always acknowledged.
Challenge the assumption that moral disagreement is always resolvable through better argument by constructing a case where it is structural and permanent.

systems_future_design.label: Systems & Future Design
prompts: Design a society that has solved loneliness as a public health problem, then show the new failure mode it created.
What does education look like when AI tutors are ubiquitous and free, but attention is the scarce resource?
Design a healthcare system optimized for prevention rather than treatment, and identify who would resist it most.
Imagine democratic governance when real-time citizen input is trivial, then describe the new bottleneck.
Design a city where no commute exceeds 15 minutes, and trace the unintended second-order effects.
Imagine a labor market where most coordination work is automated; what new social frictions appear?
Design a food distribution system in a future where weather patterns change unpredictably every 48 hours.
Imagine a legal system where AI judges are perfectly unbiased, then identify the new, massive flaw this introduces.
Design a global water governance system for a future where major aquifers have been depleted in three continents simultaneously.
Imagine a future where most physical goods are 3D printed locally, then describe the new trade networks and power structures that emerge.
Design an education system for a future where the skills needed for the job market change faster than any curriculum can adapt.
Imagine a future where a major continent has implemented universal basic income for 20 years, then trace the second-order cultural effects.
Design a healthcare system for a future where antibiotic resistance has made routine surgery life-threatening.
Imagine a future where facial recognition is perfect and universal, then design the social norms that develop to manage the loss of anonymity.
Design a city governance model for a future where 40% of the population are digital nomads who are present for only six months of the year.
Imagine a future where all journalism is AI-generated, then design the new credibility infrastructure that society would need.
Design a conflict resolution system for a future where two countries share a common AI system that makes policy recommendations to both.
Imagine a future where the human lifespan has been extended to 200 years, then trace the political consequences for democratic governance.
Design a pension system for a future where 60% of current jobs have been automated and new job categories have not yet stabilized.
Imagine a future where carbon taxes have made air travel prohibitively expensive for all but the wealthiest, then trace the effect on global culture.
Design a national language policy for a future where AI translation has eliminated the practical need to learn foreign languages.
Imagine a future where a major social media platform has been nationalized as a public utility, then trace the governance challenges.
Design a property rights system for a future where the most valuable resource is atmospheric carbon sequestration capacity.
Imagine a future where genetic screening at birth can predict disease risk with 90% accuracy, then trace the new forms of discrimination.
Design an immigration system for a future where climate displacement has made 500 million people stateless within 30 years.
Imagine a future where robots have full legal personhood, then trace what this does to the concept of labor law.
Design a system of scientific peer review for a future where AI can generate plausible but subtly incorrect scientific literature at scale.
Imagine a future where all personal data is owned by individuals and can be sold, then trace the new markets and the new poverty.
Design a global governance system for a future where three private companies control the majority of orbital infrastructure.
Imagine a future where memories can be legally deleted, then trace what this does to the criminal justice system.
Design a mental health infrastructure for a future where social media has been shown to cause measurable brain structure changes in adolescents.
Imagine a future where physical labor is entirely performed by machines but has been redefined as a luxury activity for the wealthy.
Design a system of cultural preservation for a future where AI can generate convincing historical artifacts.
Imagine a future where sleep can be compressed into two hours with pharmacological assistance, then trace the new social inequalities.
Design a geopolitical framework for a future where the Arctic has become the world's most economically productive region.
Imagine a future where political parties have been replaced by issue-based AI systems that aggregate citizen preferences in real time.
Design a food system for a future where the majority of calories consumed globally come from lab-grown protein.
Imagine a future where a single pandemic has left 30% of the global workforce with long-term cognitive impairment, then trace the economic adaptations.
Design a system of checks on AI systems that make consequential decisions in healthcare, criminal justice, and financial markets simultaneously.
Imagine a future where cities have become entirely self-sufficient in food and energy, then describe the new geopolitical relationships.
Design a system for managing collective digital inheritance when the average person dies with 50 years of behavioral data.
Imagine a future where the most scarce resource is human attention and it is traded as a commodity, then describe the new ethics of communication.
Design a governance system for a shared global AI system that is simultaneously beneficial to nations with opposing interests.
Imagine a future where the physical and digital worlds have merged completely, then trace what this does to the concept of place.
Design an electoral system for a future where credible information about candidates is impossible to distinguish from fabricated information.
Imagine a future where a major religion has formally incorporated AI as a divine interlocutor, then trace the schisms that follow.
Design a system for allocating scarce medical resources in a future where personalized medicine has made every treatment protocol unique.
Imagine a future where the nation-state has been replaced by distributed governance networks, then identify the new coordination failures.

eval_held_out: For the Gemma 4 Good Hackathon I need project ideas that feel 'wow' to judges: a sharp real-world problem, a simple story, and something Gemma 4 is genuinely good for, not just wrapping the model. Suggest several strong directions and explain why each could win.
Design a creative mentorship system for students in under-resourced schools that works offline and resists generic tutoring tropes.
Generate names for a new field of study that combines neuroscience, design thinking, and indigenous knowledge systems, but make each name imply a different worldview.
Reframe climate adaptation for coastal cities in a way that changes what gets built first and why.
Invent an educational product that becomes more useful when bandwidth gets worse, not better.
